title: Around the Alexandrov-Fenchel Inequality
speaker: Ramon Van Handel
abstract:
In the late 1800s, in the course of his study of classical problems of number theory, the young Hermann Minkowski discovered the importance of a new kind of geometric object that we now call a convex set. He soon developed a rich theory for understanding such sets, laying the foundations of convex geometry that are widely used to this day.
Among the most surprising observations of Minokwski's theory is that the classical isoperimetric theorem---which states that the ball has the smallest surface area among all bodies of a given volume---is just one special case of a much more general phenomenon. When one fixes geometric parameters other than volume, Minkowski discovered that the resulting "bubbles" can be strikingly bizarre. A complete understanding of such objects has remained a long-standing problem, with major progress being achieved only recently in joint works with Yair Shenfeld.
In this talk I will aim to discuss the history and recent progress on these questions, as well as some of their connections with other branches of mathematics including geometry, analysis, and combinatorics.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-volume Alexandrov-Fenchel setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces convex bodies, Minkowski linear combinations, mixed volumes as the nonnegative coefficients of the volume polynomial, and the Alexandrov-Fenchel inequality as log-concavity for those coefficients with n-2 reference bodies fixed.

proof_stages.label: Nontrivial equality via cap bodies
proof_stages.summary: The equality problem is motivated through the three-dimensional surface-area/mean-width/volume inequality, where Minkowski cap bodies give nonsmooth and highly nonunique minimizers unlike the usual isoperimetric picture.

proof_stages.label: Singular-limit obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Standard proofs of Alexandrov-Fenchel work in smooth positive-curvature or equal-normal-fan polytopal regimes where only trivial equality occurs, so the interesting equality cases are encoded in analytic and combinatorial singularities appearing only in limiting nonsmooth situations.

proof_stages.label: Polytopal equality classification
proof_stages.summary: For polytopal reference bodies c_1,...,c_{n-2}, Shenfeld and the speaker give a complete equality classification in terms of translation/scaling, good and bad directions determined by relative normal cones, and an additional affine-hull mechanism for lower-dimensional bodies.

proof_stages.label: Resolution and induction proof strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof removes the combinatorial singularities by perturbing only in bad directions so that projections become lower-dimensional equality cases, enabling induction through a largely linear-algebraic resolution-of-singularities argument.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial log-concavity consequences
proof_stages.summary: Stanley's mixed-volume proof of log-concavity for marked-poset linear-extension counts is revisited, and the equality classification is used to determine exactly where geometric progressions or flat plateaus can occur in such log-concave sequences.

proof_stages.label: Universality of equality mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: The closing perspective is that Alexandrov-Fenchel-type inequalities in algebraic geometry, combinatorial Hodge theory, and Lorentzian-polynomial settings should have analogous equality mechanisms, though this remains largely conjectural beyond partial progress.

examples.name: Quadratic-form sublevel ellipse
examples.note: Minkowski's passage from arithmetic quadratic-form questions to the ellipsoid where the form is less than one is presented as the historical source of the idea that convexity itself is the essential structure.
